a.name: pytest-fixtures-a
a.description: Use pytest fixtures with scopes and dependency injection to share test setup
a.body:
## Overview

Pytest fixtures provide reusable test setup via the @pytest.fixture decorator. Scopes (function, class, module, session) control when setup runs and when teardown fires.

## Usage

Declare a fixture as a function, request it by parameter name in a test, and pytest wires the value automatically. Dependencies between fixtures are resolved by pytest's dependency graph.

b.name: pytest-fixtures-b
b.description: Share pytest test setup through fixture decorators and scoped lifetimes
b.body:
## Summary

The pytest fixture system relies on the @pytest.fixture decorator to expose reusable setup helpers. Fixture scope values such as function, class, module, or session decide how often the setup function is executed.

## How it works

A test function receives fixture values by listing the fixture names as parameters; pytest inspects the signature and injects the correct values, resolving fixture-to-fixture dependencies along the way.
b.note: Same concept — fixtures, scopes, injection — two paraphrasings.

a.name: black-formatter-a
a.description: Format Python source with black, the uncompromising code formatter
a.body:
## Overview

Black rewrites Python files to a single canonical style, eliminating arguments about formatting. Line length defaults to 88 characters.

## Configuration

Settings live in pyproject.toml under [tool.black]. Common options include line-length, target-version, and skip-string-normalization.

b.name: black-formatter-b
b.description: Apply black's opinionated formatter to normalize Python style automatically
b.body:
## Purpose

The black tool reformats Python code to one fixed style so teams stop debating formatting. The default line length is 88 characters.

## Setup

Configure black through the [tool.black] section in pyproject.toml. Typical keys are line-length, target-version, and skip-string-normalization.
b.note: Same tool, same config surface, reworded.

a.name: mypy-strict-a
a.description: Enable mypy strict mode to enforce comprehensive type checking across a Python codebase
a.body:
## Overview

Mypy strict mode turns on every optional check at once: disallow-untyped-defs, warn-return-any, no-implicit-optional, and more. It is the recommended starting point for new codebases.

## Enabling

Set strict = true in the [mypy] section of mypy.ini or pyproject.toml, then gradually add ignore entries for legacy modules.

b.name: mypy-strict-b
b.description: Turn on all mypy type checks at once via strict mode for maximum type safety
b.body:
## Summary

Activating mypy's strict flag enables the full bundle of type checks simultaneously, including disallow-untyped-defs, warn-return-any, and no-implicit-optional. New projects should begin here.

## Configuration

Add strict = true under the [mypy] block in either mypy.ini or pyproject.toml, then whitelist legacy files with per-module ignores as needed.

a.name: docker-multistage-a
a.description: Reduce Docker image size with multi-stage builds that separate compile and runtime layers
a.body:
## Overview

Multi-stage builds let a Dockerfile declare several FROM statements. Only files copied into the final stage end up in the shipped image, so build tools and intermediate artifacts stay behind.

## Pattern

Compile in a builder stage, then COPY --from=builder to pull only the final binary or bundle into a slim runtime base image.

b.name: docker-multistage-b
b.description: Shrink Docker images by using multi-stage builds to leave build-time tools out of the runtime image
b.body:
## Purpose

With multi-stage Dockerfiles you can chain multiple FROM lines in one file. Only what you explicitly copy into the last stage ships with the image, keeping build dependencies out of production.

## Recipe

Use a first stage for compilation, then in the final stage use COPY --from=<stage> to import only the binary or compiled assets into a minimal base image.

a.name: k8s-secrets-a
a.description: Store sensitive configuration in Kubernetes Secrets and mount them as env vars or files
a.body:
## Overview

Kubernetes Secrets hold credentials, tokens, and keys as base64-encoded values. They can be consumed as environment variables or as files mounted into a pod's filesystem.

## Usage

Reference a Secret from a Pod spec via envFrom/env for environment variables or via a volume of type secret for file mounts.

b.name: k8s-secrets-b
b.description: Manage sensitive values in Kubernetes by using Secret objects and injecting them into pods
b.body:
## Summary

A Kubernetes Secret is an object for holding small amounts of sensitive data — passwords, API tokens, TLS keys. Values are base64-encoded and can reach pods either as environment variables or as mounted files.

## Consuming

In a Pod manifest, pull Secret values through env or envFrom to expose them as environment variables, or mount a secret volume to surface them as files inside the container.

a.name: terraform-modules-a
a.description: Package reusable Terraform infrastructure as modules with inputs and outputs
a.body:
## Overview

A Terraform module is a directory of .tf files you can call from other configurations. Modules accept variables as inputs and expose outputs the caller can consume.

## Structure

A typical module has main.tf for resources, variables.tf for inputs, and outputs.tf for returned values. Modules compose by calling other modules.

b.name: terraform-modules-b
b.description: Encapsulate Terraform resources into reusable modules exposing variables and outputs
b.body:
## Summary

In Terraform, a module is any folder of .tf files you reference from other configurations. Callers pass values in through variables and receive return values through outputs.

## Layout

The conventional shape is main.tf for resource declarations, variables.tf for declared inputs, and outputs.tf for exported values. Larger systems compose modules by calling them from each other.

a.name: react-hooks-a
a.description: Manage component state and side effects with React hooks like useState and useEffect
a.body:
## Overview

React hooks are functions starting with use that let function components use state and lifecycle features. useState manages local state; useEffect handles side effects tied to render.

## Rules

Hooks must run in the same order on every render, so call them only at the top level of a component — never inside conditionals, loops, or nested functions.

b.name: react-hooks-b
b.description: Use React hooks such as useState and useEffect inside function components for state and effects
b.body:
## Purpose

Hooks are specially-named functions that attach state and lifecycle behavior to React function components. useState gives a component local state, while useEffect ties side-effect work to the render cycle.

## Calling rules

React relies on call order, so hooks must execute in the same sequence each render. Put them at the top of a component body and avoid placing them inside if branches, loops, or helper functions.
b.note: Hooks overview and call rules, rewritten.

a.name: vue-composition-a
a.description: Build Vue 3 components with the Composition API using ref, reactive, and setup
a.body:
## Overview

The Vue 3 Composition API replaces the options object with a setup function (or <script setup>). ref creates a reactive primitive and reactive creates a reactive object.

## Pattern

Declare state with ref or reactive, derive values with computed, and react to changes with watch or watchEffect. All of this lives in setup() and returns to the template.

b.name: vue-composition-b
b.description: Adopt Vue 3's Composition API with setup, ref and reactive instead of the Options API
b.body:
## Summary

Vue 3's Composition API uses a setup function — or the <script setup> shorthand — in place of the legacy options object. Use ref for reactive primitives and reactive for reactive objects.

## Usage

Inside setup, declare reactive state with ref or reactive, use computed for derived values, and watch or watchEffect for reactions. The values returned from setup become available to the template.

a.name: fastapi-middleware-a
a.description: Attach custom middleware to a FastAPI app to intercept requests and responses
a.body:
## Overview

FastAPI middleware runs before and after every request. Use app.middleware('http') to register a coroutine that receives the request, awaits call_next, and returns a response.

## Use cases

Logging, timing, CORS, authentication, and response header injection are typical middleware jobs. Order matters — middlewares run in the order they are added.

b.name: fastapi-middleware-b
b.description: Intercept FastAPI requests and responses with custom HTTP middleware
b.body:
## Summary

Middleware in FastAPI wraps every incoming HTTP request: code before await call_next runs on the way in, and code after runs on the way out. Register one with the app.middleware('http') decorator.

## Common uses

Typical middleware jobs include request logging, timing measurements, CORS handling, auth checks, and adding response headers. Registration order determines execution order.

a.name: django-migrations-a
a.description: Generate and apply Django model migrations to evolve the database schema safely
a.body:
## Overview

Django migrations track model changes as Python files under an app's migrations/ directory. makemigrations diffs models against the last migration and writes a new file; migrate applies them to the database.

## Safety

Always review generated migrations before applying them in production. Renames and data migrations often need manual edits to avoid destructive rewrites.

b.name: django-migrations-b
b.description: Evolve Django schemas through makemigrations and migrate commands with versioned migration files
b.body:
## Summary

In Django, schema changes are captured as migration files inside each app's migrations/ folder. Running makemigrations compares current models to the last migration and generates a new file; running migrate executes the pending files against the database.

## Caveats

Read generated migrations before running them in production. Renames and data transformations usually require hand-edits to avoid data-destroying drops.

a.name: flask-blueprints-a
a.description: Organize Flask routes into Blueprint modules for modular application structure
a.body:
## Overview

Flask Blueprints group related routes, templates, and static files under a shared URL prefix. A Blueprint is declared separately and then registered on the app with app.register_blueprint.

## Structure

Split a large Flask app into multiple Blueprints — one per feature or resource — to keep the main application file small and make tests easier to scope.

b.name: flask-blueprints-b
b.description: Split a Flask project into multiple Blueprint modules to keep code organized
b.body:
## Summary

A Blueprint in Flask bundles related routes, templates, and static assets behind a common URL prefix. You create a Blueprint object and then attach it to the application using app.register_blueprint.

## Benefits

Breaking a growing Flask project into one Blueprint per feature keeps the main app file lean, improves test isolation, and lets teams own clear slices of the codebase.

a.name: redis-caching-a
a.description: Cache expensive computations and query results in Redis with TTL expiration
a.body:
## Overview

Redis works as an in-memory cache for values that are costly to recompute — database query results, API responses, or derived data. Every key can carry a TTL after which Redis evicts it automatically.

## Pattern

The cache-aside pattern: check Redis first, fall back to the source of truth on miss, then store the fresh value back into Redis with an appropriate TTL.

b.name: redis-caching-b
b.description: Use Redis as an expiring cache in front of slow data sources with a TTL per key
b.body:
## Summary

Redis serves as a fast memory cache for data that is expensive to produce — SQL query output, third-party API responses, derived views. Each key can have a time-to-live, after which Redis removes it on its own.

## Workflow

Apply cache-aside: read Redis first; on a miss, fetch from the authoritative store, then write the result back into Redis with a chosen TTL.

a.name: postgres-indexes-a
a.description: Create PostgreSQL btree and partial indexes to speed up selective queries
a.body:
## Overview

PostgreSQL's default index type is btree, suited for equality and range predicates on ordered data. Partial indexes cover only rows matching a WHERE clause, shrinking the index and keeping writes cheap.

## Usage

Use CREATE INDEX for full columns, CREATE INDEX ... WHERE ... for partials. EXPLAIN ANALYZE reveals whether a given query actually uses the index.

b.name: postgres-indexes-b
b.description: Accelerate Postgres queries by adding btree indexes and partial indexes for filtered predicates
b.body:
## Summary

In PostgreSQL, btree is the standard index and handles equality plus range comparisons over ordered values. Partial indexes cover only a subset of rows described by a WHERE clause, so they stay smaller and cost less to maintain.

## Commands

Build a regular index with CREATE INDEX and a partial with CREATE INDEX ... WHERE .... Verify the planner actually uses the index with EXPLAIN ANALYZE.

a.name: kafka-partitions-a
a.description: Choose Kafka partition counts and keys for throughput and ordering guarantees
a.body:
## Overview

Kafka topics are split into partitions; ordering is guaranteed within a partition but not across a topic. Partition count sets the ceiling on parallel consumption.

## Choosing keys

Pick a partition key that distributes load evenly while keeping related messages together — for example, a user id groups all a user's events on one partition so they are consumed in order.

b.name: kafka-partitions-b
b.description: Design Kafka topic partitioning around message keys and per-partition ordering
b.body:
## Summary

A Kafka topic is divided into partitions. Message order is preserved inside a partition, never across partitions, so the partition count caps how much parallelism a consumer group can get.

## Key selection

The partition key should spread traffic evenly while co-locating related records — using a user identifier, for instance, routes all events for that user onto one partition so they stay ordered.

a.name: lambda-cold-start-a
a.description: Reduce AWS Lambda cold start latency with provisioned concurrency and small packages
a.body:
## Overview

A Lambda cold start happens when AWS spins up a new execution environment — runtime init, dependency load, and handler first-call. Cold starts add hundreds of milliseconds to the first request.

## Mitigations

Use provisioned concurrency to keep environments warm, keep deployment packages small, and favor lightweight runtimes like Node or Python over heavy JVM-based ones.

b.name: lambda-cold-start-b
b.description: Minimize AWS Lambda cold-start latency with warm containers and slim bundles
b.body:
## Summary

Lambda cold starts occur when AWS has to create a fresh execution environment: the runtime initializes, dependencies load, and the handler is called for the first time. This can add several hundred milliseconds before the first request completes.

## Options

Keep environments warm by enabling provisioned concurrency, trim your deployment bundle, and prefer low-overhead runtimes such as Node.js or Python instead of JVM languages when latency matters.

a.name: cloud-run-a
a.description: Deploy stateless containers on Google Cloud Run with request-driven autoscaling
a.body:
## Overview

Cloud Run runs stateless HTTP containers. Google scales instances from zero to many based on concurrent request load and bills per 100ms of CPU while a request is being handled.

## Configuration

Set concurrency, min/max instances, CPU allocation, and memory per service. Cold starts are minimized by keeping min-instances above zero for latency-sensitive services.

b.name: cloud-run-b
b.description: Run stateless HTTP containers on GCP Cloud Run with autoscaling tied to traffic
b.body:
## Summary

Google Cloud Run hosts stateless HTTP containers and scales instance counts up and down according to incoming request volume, starting from zero. Billing is granular — CPU time is measured in 100ms units while a request is active.

## Knobs

Each service exposes concurrency, min-instances, max-instances, CPU, and memory settings. Setting min-instances above zero trades cost for reduced cold-start latency.

a.name: azure-functions-a
a.description: Build event-driven serverless workloads on Azure Functions with triggers and bindings
a.body:
## Overview

Azure Functions runs small handler units activated by triggers — HTTP, timer, queue, blob, event hub. Input and output bindings declaratively wire the handler to Azure services without boilerplate SDK code.

## Model

Each function has exactly one trigger and may declare additional input/output bindings. The consumption plan scales to zero; the premium plan keeps warm workers for steady traffic.

b.name: azure-functions-b
b.description: Write Azure Functions handlers invoked by triggers with declarative input/output bindings
b.body:
## Summary

Azure Functions are small pieces of code invoked when a trigger fires — HTTP call, timer tick, new queue message, blob upload, event-hub event. Bindings declare inputs and outputs from Azure services in configuration, sparing you SDK plumbing.

## Plans

A function always has one trigger and optionally additional input and output bindings. On the consumption plan, workers scale to zero; on the premium plan they stay warm for latency-sensitive workloads.

a.name: git-rebase-interactive-a
a.description: Clean up commit history with git rebase -i before pushing
a.body:
## Overview

Interactive rebase opens an editor listing every commit in a range so you can reorder, squash, fixup, or reword them. Use it to craft a clean series of commits before sharing a branch.

## Safety

Never rebase a branch that others have pulled. Rebasing rewrites commit hashes; force-pushing the rewritten history will destroy collaborators' work unless they re-base on top.

b.name: git-rebase-interactive-b
b.description: Polish a local branch's commits with git rebase -i before sharing them
b.body:
## Summary

Running git rebase -i on a commit range opens an editor where each commit can be reordered, squashed together, fixed up into another, or reworded. It's the standard way to tidy history before pushing.

## Caution

Only rebase commits that have not yet been shared. Because rebase rewrites hashes, force-pushing the result over a branch others already pulled will clobber their work unless they reset onto the new base.

a.name: gha-matrix-a
a.description: Run GitHub Actions workflows across multiple OS and language versions with matrix builds
a.body:
## Overview

A matrix strategy in GitHub Actions fans out one job into many variants, each with a different combination of dimensions such as OS, language version, or feature flag. Every cell runs in parallel.

## Syntax

Under the job's strategy.matrix key, list dimensions as arrays. Actions expands the cross-product into separate jobs and surfaces each as its own run in the checks UI.

b.name: gha-matrix-b
b.description: Expand a single GitHub Actions job into parallel runs across a matrix of configurations
b.body:
## Summary

GitHub Actions' matrix strategy turns one job definition into multiple parallel runs, each covering a different combination of axes — operating systems, runtime versions, feature toggles. The runner launches every combination concurrently.

## Declaring it

Under strategy.matrix, declare each dimension as an array. Actions computes the full cross-product of all dimensions and spawns one job per cell, reporting each as its own check.

a.name: jwt-auth-a
a.description: Authenticate API requests using signed JSON Web Tokens passed in the Authorization header
a.body:
## Overview

A JWT is a signed token carrying claims — subject, issuer, expiry. Clients send it in the Authorization: Bearer <token> header; servers verify the signature and trust the claims inside.

## Tradeoffs

JWTs are stateless so servers don't need session storage, but revocation is harder — short lifetimes plus refresh tokens are the standard mitigation.

b.name: jwt-auth-b
b.description: Secure REST endpoints with JWT bearer tokens signed and verified on each request
b.body:
## Summary

JSON Web Tokens are signed payloads containing claims such as subject, issuer, and expiration. A client attaches the token to every request via the Authorization: Bearer <token> header; the server validates the signature and then trusts the claims.

## Pros and cons

Because JWTs are self-contained, the server avoids session storage. The downside is that revoking a valid token is awkward — the usual fix is short-lived access tokens paired with refresh tokens.

a.name: oauth2-flow-a
a.description: Implement the OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow with PKCE for single-page apps
a.body:
## Overview

The authorization code flow with PKCE protects public clients — SPAs and mobile apps — that cannot safely hold a client secret. The browser sends a code verifier hash, then exchanges the code for tokens.

## Steps

Redirect to the authorization endpoint with a PKCE challenge, receive a code on the callback, then POST the code plus verifier to the token endpoint to obtain access and refresh tokens.

b.name: oauth2-flow-b
b.description: Use OAuth 2.0 authorization code + PKCE to sign users in from a browser app
b.body:
## Summary

For public clients like single-page or mobile apps that can't keep a client secret, the authorization code flow with PKCE adds a per-request challenge so intercepted codes can't be redeemed. The browser hashes a random verifier and sends only the hash until the final exchange.

## Sequence

The app redirects to the authorize endpoint carrying a PKCE challenge; the user consents; the provider redirects back with a code; the app POSTs the code and original verifier to the token endpoint to receive access and refresh tokens.

a.name: api-versioning-a
a.description: Version REST APIs via URL path prefixes like /v1/ to evolve without breaking clients
a.body:
## Overview

Path-based versioning places the major version in the URL — /v1/users, /v2/users. Each version is a stable contract; breaking changes bump the prefix and old versions stay available until deprecation.

## Alternatives

Header-based and media-type versioning keep URLs clean but are harder to inspect in logs and CDN rules. Path versioning wins on operability.

b.name: api-versioning-b
b.description: Use URL path prefixes for REST API versioning so breaking changes land in a new major version
b.body:
## Summary

With path-based versioning the major version lives in the URL: /v1/users and /v2/users are different endpoints. The contract for any given version stays frozen; new major versions live beside old ones until the old version is retired.

## Compared with other options

Header-based and media-type versioning keep URLs prettier but make log inspection and CDN rule writing harder. In practice path versioning is the most operationally friendly choice.

a.name: graphql-pagination-a
a.description: Paginate GraphQL lists using the Relay cursor connection specification
a.body:
## Overview

The Relay cursor connection pattern replaces offset/limit with opaque cursors. A connection exposes edges (node + cursor) and a pageInfo block with hasNextPage and endCursor.

## Query shape

Clients pass first/after or last/before arguments; servers decode the cursor to locate the boundary record and return the next slice plus updated pageInfo values.

b.name: graphql-pagination-b
b.description: Follow the Relay connection spec to paginate GraphQL lists with opaque cursors
b.body:
## Summary

Relay-style cursor pagination in GraphQL replaces numeric offsets with opaque cursors. Every connection returns a list of edges — each edge holds a node and its cursor — plus a pageInfo object carrying hasNextPage and endCursor.

## Arguments

A client asks for first/after to page forward or last/before to page backward. The server decodes the cursor, finds the boundary record, and returns the next slice along with refreshed pageInfo.

a.name: webpack-splitting-a
a.description: Split Webpack bundles by route or vendor to reduce initial JavaScript payload
a.body:
## Overview

Webpack code splitting breaks a single bundle into multiple chunks loaded on demand. Dynamic import() produces a lazy chunk; splitChunks extracts shared code into common vendor bundles.

## Strategy

Split at route boundaries so each page loads only its own code, and extract long-lived dependencies like React into a vendor chunk cached separately.

b.name: webpack-splitting-b
b.description: Shrink the initial JS bundle by applying Webpack's code-splitting features per route and vendor
b.body:
## Summary

Webpack code splitting turns one big bundle into several smaller chunks that load only when needed. Calling import() asynchronously creates a separate chunk; the splitChunks plugin hoists shared modules into common vendor bundles.

## Approach

Split at the route level so each page only downloads its own code, and isolate stable dependencies such as React into a vendor bundle that can be cached across deploys.

a.name: vite-build-a
a.description: Configure Vite's Rollup-based production build for optimal output size and caching
a.body:
## Overview

Vite uses esbuild in dev and Rollup in production. The production build applies tree-shaking, code splitting, and content-hashed filenames so aggressive long-term caching is safe.

## Tuning

Set build.target to the minimum supported browser, enable build.minify with esbuild for speed or terser for smaller output, and use build.rollupOptions for advanced chunking.

b.name: vite-build-b
b.description: Tune Vite's production bundle via Rollup options for size, caching, and browser targets
b.body:
## Summary

Development in Vite is powered by esbuild, while the production build is produced by Rollup. That build does tree-shaking, code splitting, and content-hashed filenames, making long-term caching safe.

## Configuration

Pick a build.target matching your browser baseline, choose build.minify (esbuild is faster, terser compresses more), and reach for build.rollupOptions when you need custom chunking.

a.name: jest-mocking-a
a.description: Mock modules and functions in Jest with jest.mock and jest.fn to isolate units under test
a.body:
## Overview

jest.mock replaces an entire module with a stub — either auto-mocked or with a factory. jest.fn creates a standalone mock function that tracks calls and can be given a return value or implementation.

## Usage

Call jest.mock at the top of the test file before imports. For per-test behavior, adjust the mock inside beforeEach or by calling mockReturnValueOnce within the test itself.

b.name: jest-mocking-b
b.description: Use jest.mock and jest.fn to stub modules and functions for isolated Jest unit tests
b.body:
## Summary

The jest.mock helper swaps out an entire module — it can auto-generate mocks or accept a factory. jest.fn returns a fresh mock function that records every call and lets you set its return value or implementation.

## How to apply

Place jest.mock at the top of the test file so it hoists above imports. To vary behavior per test, reconfigure the mock inside a beforeEach block or use mockReturnValueOnce directly in the test body.

a.name: cypress-e2e-a
a.description: Write end-to-end browser tests in Cypress with automatic waiting and retriable assertions
a.body:
## Overview

Cypress runs inside the browser and automatically retries commands until they succeed or a timeout fires. Tests read as cy.visit, cy.get, cy.click, cy.should and compose into linear user journeys.

## Patterns

Prefer data-testid selectors over CSS classes; use cy.intercept to stub network calls; group related tests in describe blocks with shared beforeEach setup.

b.name: cypress-e2e-b
b.description: Build Cypress end-to-end tests that mirror user flows with auto-retrying commands
b.body:
## Summary

Cypress is a browser-based testing tool whose commands auto-retry until the assertion passes or the timeout elapses. Each spec chains cy.visit, cy.get, cy.click, and cy.should calls into a straightforward walk through the UI.

## Recommended practices

Select elements with stable data-testid attributes rather than CSS classes; stub outbound calls with cy.intercept; organize related specs under describe blocks that share setup via beforeEach.

a.name: playwright-selectors-a
a.description: Pick Playwright locators with getByRole, getByTestId, and getByText for stable selectors
a.body:
## Overview

Playwright's locator API exposes user-facing strategies first: getByRole and getByText match the way humans identify elements. getByTestId targets an explicit test attribute for structural cases.

## Guidance

Prefer role-based locators over CSS or XPath because they resist markup changes. Fall back to a test id when no semantic role fits, and avoid text matches on translated content.

b.name: playwright-selectors-b
b.description: Use Playwright's semantic locators (getByRole, getByText, getByTestId) for resilient tests
b.body:
## Summary

Playwright's locator API prioritizes user-visible strategies. getByRole and getByText mirror how a real user names an element; getByTestId is reserved for cases where no good semantic role exists.

## Recommendations

Role-based locators survive most markup refactors, so use them first. Use a test id only when no role fits, and avoid locating by translated text since copy changes break tests across locales.

a.name: grpc-streaming-a
a.description: Stream data between gRPC clients and servers using server-, client-, and bidirectional streams
a.body:
## Overview

gRPC supports four RPC shapes: unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, and bidirectional-streaming. Streaming methods use repeated messages over a single HTTP/2 connection.

## Picking a shape

Use server-streaming for paged or tail-follow responses, client-streaming for large uploads, and bidirectional for interactive sessions like chat or live telemetry.

b.name: grpc-streaming-b
b.description: Choose gRPC streaming modes — server, client, and bidirectional — for different data shapes
b.body:
## Summary

gRPC ships four RPC styles: plain unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, and full bidirectional streaming. All of them run over a single HTTP/2 stream, with streaming methods exchanging many messages instead of one.

## When to use each

Server-streaming fits paginated reads and tail-follow feeds. Client-streaming is for large uploads. Bidirectional streaming suits two-way conversations — chat, live telemetry, interactive tools.

a.name: sqlalchemy-session-a
a.description: Manage SQLAlchemy Session lifecycle with context managers and per-request scoping
a.body:
## Overview

The SQLAlchemy Session is the unit-of-work that stages changes and flushes them on commit. A Session must be short-lived — typically one per request or one per task — and always closed in a finally block or context manager.

## Pattern

Open a Session at the start of a logical operation, commit on success or roll back on error, and close it at the end. Never share a Session across threads or long-running tasks.

b.name: sqlalchemy-session-b
b.description: Scope SQLAlchemy Sessions per request and commit or roll back inside a context manager
b.body:
## Summary

A SQLAlchemy Session is a unit-of-work that accumulates object changes and pushes them to the database on commit. Sessions should live briefly — one per web request or background task — and must always be closed, ideally via a context manager or finally clause.

## Usage

Create the Session at the start of the operation, commit when everything succeeds, roll back on exceptions, and close when done. Never share one Session across threads or long-lived processes.
